data.title: Peloton: Gym Expansion And Cost Cuts Anchor This Rebound Story (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I'm watching the rapid bounce back in the S&P 500 with a large degree of apprehension. Even though China tariffs have been put on hold, there's still a dark cloud over the U.S. economy, which we can already see via a pullback in consumer spending (retail and restaurant companies have reported vastly weaker same-store sales growth this earnings quarter; travel companies have cited weaker demand and airlines are cutting capacity). Against that backdrop, I continue to favor smaller-cap stocks that have independent catalysts to outperform the broader market.

Peloton (NASDAQ:PTON) is one such play. Though the fitness equipment maker has fallen sharply from grace, the company's new direction under new CEO Peter Stern, the former head of Apple Fitness+, is promising. Shares of Peloton have sunk ~20% since the start of the year even though the company has shored up its profitability, creating what I believe to be a well-timed buying opportunity.

I last wrote a neutral, cautious opinion on Peloton in February, when the stock was still trading in the mid-$8s. Since then, a number of factors have swung in the positive direction for this company. For one, the company reported excellent Q3 results that finally showed stabilization in subscriber counts, alongside a healthy rise in gross margins and overall profitability that led Peloton to lift its full-year adjusted EBITDA expectations. Second, the company has quantified and confirmed the de minimis impact of tariffs (a $5 million negative FCF headwind in fiscal Q4) on its business, covering only the aluminum used in Peloton and Precor-branded equipment as well as immaterial apparel purchases from China. With this in mind, I've turned cautiously optimistic on Peloton, and I am upgrading the stock to a buy rating.

To me, these are the core reasons that make me more sanguine on Peloton's prospects:

  • Enterprise business development opportunities. It's true that Peloton may be losing momentum as a direct-to-consumer brand, as evidenced by its falling hardware sales. However, the company has recently elevated a new Chief Commercial Officer to oversee a number of initiatives, including selling Pelotons directly to gyms and hotels. The company recently released a new collection of on-demand workouts across 2,400 Hilton locations.
  • Running, strength, and men. Despite recent subscriber declines, the company has seen meaningful increases in non-rowing workouts, especially running and strength, which is also bringing in a larger male demographic to Peloton's user base. In my view, Peloton's strategy to lean into these more comprehensive workout offerings can broaden its customer base and give the company a chance to reset its brand.
  • Committed to profitability. Peloton has slashed expenses dramatically over the past few years, leading to meaningful adjusted EBITDA growth as well as slimming down its debt profile. In my view, the company's stabilizing top-line trends on top of its improving profitability and slimming debt load makes the company far more appealing to a potential private equity buyer.

By far, valuation remains one of the most compelling reasons to invest in this stock. At current share prices just under $7, Peloton trades at a market cap of $2.70 billion. After we net off the $914.3 million of cash and $1.50 billion of total debt on Peloton's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $3.29 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY25 (which ends in June 2025), Peloton has made several positive tweaks to its outlook. After outperforming on paid subscribers in Q3, the company lifted its year-end paid subscription guidance to 2.77-2.79 million members, 20k higher at the low end versus its prior outlook. It also raised its adjusted EBITDA outlook to a $340 million midpoint (14% margin), 5% higher versus the midpoint of the prior $300-$350 million range even after incorporating tariff impacts in Q4.

This positions Peloton's valuation at just 9.7x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA, for a business that is currently growing adjusted EBITDA at a >50% y/y pace. In my view, the company has plenty of levers for double-digit adjusted EBITDA expansion ahead in FY26, including a higher degree of commercial gym/hotel sales, subscriber stabilization, and flow-through from the recent trend of higher hardware gross margins. The single-digit adjusted EBITDA multiple, to me, is a great value-oriented entry point: and it also increases the likelihood of private equity interest in the name (where possible buyers have been rumored to circle Peloton since mid-2024).

While Peloton is certainly not without its risks, the stock has now fallen to very buyable levels. Take advantage of the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q3 download

Let's now go through Peloton's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The fiscal Q3 (March quarter) earnings summary is shown below:

Peloton's revenue declined -13% y/y to $624.0 million, driven in particular by a -27% y/y decide in hardware revenue but coming in ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $621.7 million. Meanwhile, we continue to see signs of encouragement on the subscription side, where the company added total paid Connected Fitness subscribers in the quarter to end with 2.88 million subs, breaking a recent trend of declines. We note that the company's guidance for Q4/FY25 still calls for as slight seasonal decline in Q4 (as historically the company has noted more members churn during the warmer summer months), but we note that the fact that the company is emphasizing different types of workouts (running, strength) may help Peloton to break from prior seasonal norms. We note as well that average churn rates improved 20bps to 1.2%, versus 1.4% in Q2.

Per CEO Peter Stern's remarks on the Q3 earnings call on member engagement:

We observed 5% year-over-year growth in running workouts and 11% growth in walking workouts. Engagement with newer features such as Pace Targets on our treadmill continued to improve. In Q3, over 80% of Tread users taking a running workout used Pace Targets, up from just under 60% last quarter.

We see further opportunities to keep winning cardio by delivering innovative software, hardware and even more engaging content. We'll share more about our innovation roadmap later this calendar year.

Turning to holistic wellness. In Q3, we saw a higher mix of workout time in strength disciplines quarter-over-quarter and year-over-year. We also delivered new kettlebell content in late February and nearly 70,000 members completed kettlebell workouts by the end of the quarter. Beyond strength, meditation classes taken increased 7% year-over-year in the third quarter."

The company noted as well that marketing outreach to members at risk of churn helped to improve subscriber retention rates above the company's initial expectations. And yet even despite this, the company still managed to heavily slash spending, particularly in sales and marketing, which declined -38% y/y to $106 million in the third quarter. The company also cut down R&D spending by -22% y/y, driven by headcount reductions.

On top of a 780bps y/y boost in gross margins (driven by a higher hardware gross margin, plus a more favorable revenue mix toward subscriptions), Peloton was able to boost adjusted EBITDA to $89.4 million (a 14.3% margin), which grew 53% sequentially and was meaningfully better than a near-breakeven $5.8 million adjusted EBITDA in the year-ago Q3.

We note as well that Peloton has jumped to a ~$100 million FCF run rate over the past few quarters, which it has directed into a hefty deleveraging of its net debt position:

Going forward, I expect that Peloton's increasing mix of sales toward enterprise locations (gyms, hotels, universities) may help the company to stabilize its overall churn rates and build a base for continued adjusted EBITDA margin improvement.

Risks and key takeaways

It probably goes without saying that Peloton's aggressive attempts at a turnaround comes laden with a number of risks, which include:

  • Competition amid weaker consumer spending. Peloton is still positioning itself as the premium fitness brand, and amid macroeconomic headwinds, customers may choose to buy cheaper alternatives like NordicTrack.
  • Memberships have yet to return to meaningful growth. Though Peloton appears to have stabilized its near-term churn, it still has a ways to go until it can show meaningful member growth again, which outside a potential takeover offer is the main path back to improved valuation multiples.

Still, I believe these risks are more than encapsulated into Peloton's bargain-basement <10x forward adjusted EBITDA multiples. In my view, it's time to stake a small position here and bank on a broader rebound.

data.title: Tracking Akre Capital Management Portfolio - Q1 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Akre Capital Management’s 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Akre’s regulatory 13F Form filed on 05/13/2025. Please visit our Tracking Akre Capital Management Portfolio series to get an idea of their investment philosophy and our last update for their moves during Q4 2024.

This quarter, Akre’s 13F portfolio value decreased ~10% from ~$11.56B to ~$10.40B. The number of positions remained steady at 18. The largest five stakes are MasterCard, O’Reilly Automotive, Moody’s, Visa, and KKR. They account for ~61% of the total portfolio value.

AUM is distributed among private funds, separately managed accounts, and the Akre Focus Fund (MUTF:AKREX) (AKRIX). Since the 2009 inception through Q1 2025, the mutual fund’s annualized returns are at 14.93% compared to 13.71% for the S&P 500 Index. For 2024, the fund returned 1.46% compared to negative 4.27% for the S&P 500 Index. Their cash allocation is currently at ~6.5% - it is up from ~1.4% as of last quarter. Akre coined the term "compounding machines" to describe the types of businesses they invest in. To learn more about that investing style, check out the book '100 to 1 in the Stock Market'.

Note: positions in the mutual fund not reported through the 13F filings include Constellation Software (OTCPK:CNSWF), LVMH (OTCPK:LVMUY), and Topicus.com (OTCPK:TOITF). Topicus.com is a spinoff from Constellation Software.

Stake Increases:

Brookfield Corp. (BN): BN is a 9.62% of the portfolio stake established in Q3 2019 at prices between $20 and $24 and increased by ~18% next quarter at prices between $22 and $27. The first three quarters of 2020 saw another ~45% increase at prices between $10 and $33.50. That was followed with another ~30% stake increase during Q4 2022 at prices between ~$31 and ~$35. Since then, there have only been minor adjustments. The stock currently trades at $59.53.

Note: the prices quoted above are adjusted for the Asset Management Business spinoff completed in December 2022. Shares of the spinoff, which assumed the name Brookfield Asset Management, were distributed at a ratio of 1 share of the separated business for every 4 Class A shares held. The parent company was then renamed Brookfield Corp.

Airbnb (ABNB): ABNB is a 3.35% of the portfolio position established during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$141 and ~$165. The next quarter saw a ~180% stake increase at prices between ~$113 and ~$154. That was followed by a ~10% further increase this quarter at prices between ~$116 and ~$164. The stock currently trades at ~$138.

CCC Intelligent Solutions (CCCS): The very small 1.15% CCCS position was purchased during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$10.50 and ~$12.50. The next quarter saw a ~12% stake increase at prices between $10.55 and $11.98. That was followed by a ~37% stake increase during Q3 2024 at prices between $9.97 and $11.33. This quarter also saw a ~24% stake increase at prices between $8.68 and $11.80. The stock is now at $9.04.

Stake Decreases:

Mastercard (MA): MA is currently the largest 13F stake at ~18% of the portfolio. It was first purchased in 2010 and has consistently increased since. Their overall cost-basis is ~$22 compared to the current price of ~$573 per share. Q1 2020 saw a ~9% stake increase, while Q2 2022 saw a ~14% selling at prices between ~$309 and ~$379. That was followed by a ~40% reduction in the three quarters through Q2 2024 at prices between ~$365 and ~$489. This quarter also saw a ~10% selling at prices between ~$500 and ~$582. They are harvesting gains.

O'Reilly Automotive (ORLY): ORLY is a large ~12% portfolio stake first purchased in 2005. Over 800K shares were purchased at the time. The position was at 825K shares as of Q1 2017 - every year had seen adjustments but overall, the stake had remained remarkably steady over that twelve-year period – the stock returned ~10x during that time. 2017 saw a ~130% stake increase at an average cost of ~$195. Q1 2021 saw a ~14% selling at prices between ~$425 and ~$509. There was a ~20% selling during Q4 2022 at prices between ~$703 and ~$865. The stock is now at ~$1328. The last two years also saw a ~37% selling. They are harvesting gains.

Moody's Corporation (MCO): The initial purchase of MCO happened in Q1 & Q2 2012 in the high-30s price-range. Recent activity follows: There was a ~18% trimming in the three quarters through Q2 2024. The next quarter saw a ~20% further reduction at prices between ~$421 and ~$495. The last quarter also saw a ~8% trimming at prices between $453.23 and $500.88. That was followed by a ~21% reduction this quarter at prices between ~$436 and ~$532. It currently trades at ~$480 and is now at ~11% of the portfolio. They are harvesting gains.

Note: Akre’s cost-basis on MCO is ~$39.

Visa Inc. (V): Visa is a ~11% of the portfolio stake. It was established in Q2 2012 at a cost-basis of around $30. Q2 2013 saw a one-third increase in the low-40s and that was followed with a 60% increase in Q2 2016 in the high 70s. Q1 2017 also saw another roughly one-third increase at prices between $79.50 and $90. Q4 2023 saw a ~27% selling at prices between ~$229 and ~$262. The stock is now at ~$356. There was a ~3% trimming this quarter.

Note: Visa stock split 4-for-1 in March 2015. The prices quoted above are adjusted for that split.

KKR & Co. (KKR): KKR is a ~10% of the portfolio position established in Q1 2018 at prices between $20 and $24.50 and doubled next quarter at prices between $19.50 and $25.50. There was another ~25% increase in Q4 2018 at prices between $18.50 and $28. The first three quarters of 2020 also saw a ~15% increase. That was followed by a ~10% increase during Q3 2023 between ~$54 and ~$65. H1 2024 saw a ~20% trimming. That was followed by a ~17% reduction during the next quarter at prices between ~$104 and ~$133. The last quarter also saw a ~10% selling at prices between $129.70 and $162.87. There was a further ~14% trimming this quarter at prices between ~$105 and ~$170. The stock currently trades at ~$129. They are harvesting gains.

Note: KKR converted from a partnership to a corporation effective July 1, 2018.

Roper Technologies (ROP): ROP is an 8.22% of the portfolio position, first purchased in Q2 & Q3 2014 at prices between $129 and $150. The stake saw incremental buying in the following quarters. In Q2 2016, there was a ~20% increase at prices between $165 and $185. The first three quarters of 2020 saw a ~22% stake increase at prices between $254 and $453. H1 2021 saw a ~12% trimming, and that was followed by a ~5% trimming during Q3 2023. There was minor trimming in the last six quarters. The stock is now at ~$566.

CoStar Group (CSGP): The ~7% CSGP stake was purchased in Q4 2019 at prices between ~$54 and ~$62 and increased by ~75% in the next quarter at prices between ~$52 and ~$74. Q2 2020 saw another ~120% stake increase at prices between ~$53 and ~$73. There was a ~25% reduction in Q2 2022 at prices between ~$54 and ~$68. Q2 2024 saw a ~20% stake increase at prices between $72.73 and $95.69. The stock currently trades at $75.96. There were minor increases during the last two quarters, while during this quarter there was marginal trimming.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for the 10-for-1 stock split in June 2022.

American Tower (AMT): AMT has been in the portfolio for over two decades. It is now at 5.42% of the portfolio. Recent activity follows: the six years through Q3 2018 saw consistent buying almost every quarter. The share count increased over four times from 1.7M shares to 7.1M shares during that period. The buying happened at prices between $65 and $118. The stake was decreased by 9% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$185 and ~$217. That was followed by a ~9% trimming last quarter at prices between $177.82 and $228.37. There was a ~53% selling this quarter at prices between ~$173 and ~$220. The stock is now at ~$204. They are harvesting gains.

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), CarMax Inc. (KMX), DigitalBridge (DBRG), and Verisk Analytics (VRSK): These small (less than ~1% of the portfolio each) stakes were reduced during the quarter.

Kept Steady:

Danaher Corporation (DHR): DHR is a 3.50% of the portfolio position established in Q4 2013 at prices between $47 and $55 and increased by roughly five-times in Q2 2014 at prices between $52 and $59. Since then, the activity has been minor. The stock is now at ~$188.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for the Fortive spinoff in July 2017.

Goosehead Insurance (GSHD), and SOPHiA GENETICS SA (SOPH): These small (less than ~0.5% of the portfolio each) stakes were kept steady this quarter.

Below is a spreadsheet that highlights the changes to Akre Capital Management’s 13F stock portfolio as of Q1 2025:

Source: John Vincent. Data constructed from Akre Capital Management’s 13F filings for Q4 2024 and Q1 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Mercury General: Rebuilding Its Capital Quickly After LA Wildfires
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I remain bullish on Mercury General (NYSE:MCY) because the core business continues to generate underwriting profits and regulatory tailwinds are also now translating into double-digit rate increases. The company has also rebuilt its capital base faster than the market expected after January's LA fires. The underlying combined ratios for personal auto and homeowners stood at 92.1% and 76.1%, respectively, in 2024, and those run-rate margins persisted into 1Q 2025 even though headline results were distorted by catastrophic losses [CAT]. With other competitors such as State Farm (STFGX) and Allstate (ALL) pulling back from California, MCY's disciplined underwriting and deep agent network position it to capture incremental share at attractive prices.

I previously covered Mercury General here.

Earnings Check-in

When I last wrote on Mercury General on 1st April 2025, I argued that the wildfire hit was manageable. 1Q 2025 earnings results confirmed that view. Net premiums earned grew almost 10% y/y to $1.28 Bn while investment income jumped 25% to $81 Mn. Statutory surplus only slipped to $1.82 Bn from the record $2.03 Bn posted at the end of 2024. The quarter showed a GAAP net loss of $108 Mn and a combined ratio of 119.2%. Of course, the combined ratio is above 100% due to the CAT losses, which added 28 points. However, these figures sit squarely in management's previously guided $155-$325Mn pre-tax range. The board still declared the usual $0.3175 dividend, signaling no stress on capital.

Business Overview

Mercury writes roughly 64% of its net premiums in personal auto, 22% in homeowners and the remainder in small commercial lines across 11 states. Yet more than 80% of all businesses are California-based. That concentration looked like a liability during the fires, but the departure of national carriers has now turned it into an advantage. In February 2025, the California Department of Insurance (DOI) cleared a 12.3% average homeowners rate hike (and mid-single-digit bumps on condos and renters). This decision ended up affecting over 600K policies beginning in March 2025. The same DOI reform package lets carriers include reinsurance costs and catastrophe-model outputs in future filings if they keep writing in high-risk zones. And this is something Mercury already does, so I see additional pricing power flowing through 2026.

Growth and Profitability Catalysts

First, the approved homeowners hike alone should lift earned-premium yield roughly 5 points over the next 12–18 months as policies renew. Secondly, MCY's fixed income reinvestment rate now exceeds 5%, driving a 25% jump in investment income this quarter. This is worth about $0.7 of annual [EPS] by 2026 if rates stay flat. Thirdly, management is aggressively pursuing subrogation on the Eaton fire, and they publicly estimate a 40-70% recovery. This means that capital will drop straight to surplus once settled. Finally, AI-enabled claims automation has already shaved 10% off cycle times, and I expect that to trim at least 200 bps from the expense ratio by 2026. This adds to the fact that MCY already has a decent growth profile relative to its sector.

Valuation

Because near-term GAAP earnings are fogged by wildfire charges, I will focus on normalized 2026 EPS. If you remove the one-off wildfire from 1Q 2025, so that the run rate reflects MCY normal economics, it yields a normalized blended combined ratio of 95% for 2026, which is 2 points higher than the pre-COVID average, to stay conservative. The 12.3% rate hike approved in March 2025 flows through policy renewals over 12 months. And that alone adds about 4% to consolidated earned premium.

If we also assume a 2% unit growth driven by the departure of other national carriers, this will yield 6% top-line growth in both 2025 and 2026. This means that the 2026 net premiums earned should be ~$5.63 Bn.

If I apply a 95% combined ratio to the $5.63 Bn of net earned premiums, I get $282Mn underwriting profit, which is a 5% margin of total revenues. Additionally, the investable asset base was $5.4 Bn as of 31 March 2025. Cash flows from the growing book expand the base at 2% per year, so I arrive at $5.6 Bn for 2026. Consequently, this should yield $280 Mn of net investment income after expenses.

Cumulatively, this yields a total of $562 Mn EBIT and $422 Mn of net income after taxes. If I divide the $422 Mn with shares outstanding of 55.4 Mn, I arrive at a 2026 fully diluted EPS of $7.60. To be conservative, we trim out a 15% for possible weather volatility, etc. and this gets to $6.50.

Similar peers in the industry, such as Progressive (PGR), Travelers (TRV) and Allstate trade at 17x-19x FWD earnings. Pure-play California auto carrier CSAA Mutuals change hands near 14x. Therefore, I will choose 15x, which is a slight discount to its peers to account for Mercury's single-state concentration yet still reward its above-peer growth. This yields an equity value of $98.

To reinforce my thesis, let's confirm this with book value. GAAP shareholders' equity was $1.82 Bn on 31 March 2025, which is ~32.9 per share. This means that if the figure is rolled forward by adding 2025 and 2026 retained earnings after dividends, it yields a 2026 year-end book of $2.41Bn, or $43 per share. Generally, P&C carriers with ROEs above 12% usually command 2x-2.3x book. Therefore, if I select the lower-end multiple of 2x, it produces an equity value of $86.

Because earnings capitalize future profitability while P/Book anchors the valuation to tangible surplus, weighting 60% P/E and 40% P/Book yields a final price of $90. At the current $60 price, Mercury trades at 8.6x normalized [EPS] and 1.4x FWD Book, both deep discounts to the regional P&C cohort.

Mercury's Key Risks I am watching

As I had mentioned in my previous article on Mercury, there is a large risk that another large California wildfire could erupt before surplus is rebuilt. And this would pressure capital and ratings. Additionally, DOI delays on further rate filings could slow margin recovery. I also believe that July 1 reinsurance will likely be up and if the market hardens beyond expectations, the ceded premium cost could add 1–2 points to the combined ratio. Also, higher interest rates could pull fair-value marks on the bond portfolio lower, depressing reported equity.

Final Thoughts

The market is still pricing Mercury at barely 9x normalized FWD earnings, despite evidence that wildfire losses are finite. Additionally, subrogation recoveries are material and the rate of momentum is increasing. Raymond James has already moved to a strong buy with an $80 target. In my view, the combination of improving pricing power, the shrinking competition and swift capital build creates a great risk/reward profile for Mercury General.

data.title: JPIB: An Attractive International Bond Fund From JPMorgan
data.description:
Thesis

JPMorgan International Bond Opportunities ETF (BATS:JPIB) is a fixed income exchange traded fund. As per its objective, the fund:

Invests in bond and currency sectors across developed and emerging markets outside the U.S. without benchmark constraints. Designed to deliver total return from a portfolio that is diversified across international bond markets.

The fund comes from the JPMorgan platform, and benefits from the extensive research capabilities presented by the company. In today's article we are going to look at the fund composition, the risk factors for the fund and its metrics, and highlight why JPIB is a robust addition to a balanced portfolio.

What type of bonds does the fund own? Mostly investment grade

As per its objective, the fund invests in international bonds. When we look at the country allocation, we get a very diversified picture:

The fund has a very well balanced approach, with no jurisdiction outside of the U.S. representing more than 11% of the fund. Please note there is a heavy EM allocation in this portfolio.

From a ratings standpoint the fund is mostly investment grade:

AAA bonds make up 15% of the fund, followed by A at 18% and BBB at 24.5%. The sub-investment grade sleeve is roughly 35%, mostly comprised of BB names.

The ETF represents a mix of corporate and government exposure:

There is no defining sleeve here, with the fund evenly mixed in terms of governments versus corporates. To get a better sense of what the components are, let us have a look at the largest exposures currently:

  • FEDERATIVE 10% 01/27: 2.9% of the fund. This is a Brazil government bond denominated in local currency.
  • CANADA BOND 4% 08/26: 2.33% of the fund. This is a Canada government bond denominated in local currency.

Despite some of the largest exposures currently being in local currency, most of the fund contains assets denominated in Usd, with the currency split as follows:

  • Usd: 93.2%
  • Other: residual

This translates into a weaker dollar having only a moderate positive effect on this name.

Other analytics

  • AUM: $0.7
  • Expense ratio: 0.5%
  • Yield: 5.18%
  • St Dev: 5.7%
  • Duration: 5 years
  • Portfolio YTM: 6.7%

Comparison versus peers

The parsing issues in the global bond space have to do with hard currency versus local currency, and government bonds versus corporate bonds. JPIB is very much a blend, and it targets the intermediate portion of the yield curve:

We have composed a cohort which includes the iShares JPMorgan USD EM Bond ETF (EMB), the Invesco EM Sovereign Debt ETF (PCY) and the Global X EM Bond ETF (EMBD). As we can observe from the above chart, JPIB has the lowest volatility from the cohort, consistent with its low duration and robust built. Kindly note the lower credit risk run by JPIB via its robust performance during the April 2025 market melt-down. The cohort of peers exhibits very deep drawdowns, especially during periods of credit spread widening. Let us see if they get compensated for the higher risk via their coupons:

PCY and EMBD do indeed have higher total returns on a 3-year basis, but let us also compare Sharpe ratios, which embed risk vs returns:

  • JPIB: 0.01
  • EMB: 0.06
  • PCY: 0.11
  • EMBD: 0.16

All funds have very low Sharpe ratios, indicating only a modest yield coverage for the risks they are taking.

Risk factors

The main risk factor for this fund is represented by U.S. rates:

The name rallied almost +3% in September 2024 when the yield curve shifted lower, while being only modestly affected by he April 2025 market meltdown which had only a -1.2% impact on the name. Since the portfolio is overweight hard currency bonds, the U.S. rates risk factor is the most important one. Via its credit profile we saw the fund is mostly investment grade, hence why credit spreads have a secondary impact here.

A global recession with a significant risk-off environment can however put downward pressure on the name, with a potential drawdown of -5% from credit spreads. The fairly short duration and robust composition will mitigate to a certain extent the down move.

Low risk EM exposure fund

JPIB is a low risk EM fund with a robust IG build. Given its composition and portfolio yield, do not expect HY returns here. A 5% yield plus a 2% duration impact is a fair assessment for a normalized market. We can see the same profile from its historic figures:

Its worst year was in 2022 when higher rates and wider credit spreads contributed to the negative performance. The fund is off to a good start in 2025, with a rough +3% total return, despite the recent market turmoil. In a normalized environment with no exogenous surprises, expect a +7% return in 2025 as described in the analysis above.

While JPIB is not 'exciting' per se, it does provide for a reliable high total return in today's high rates environment, and also acts as a portfolio diversifier.

Conclusion

JPIB is a fixed income ETF. The fund comes from JPMorgan and represents an international bond fund with an intermediate duration. Most of the assets are Usd (i.e. over 90%) hence the fund does not take currency risk, nor will it benefit greatly from a weaker dollar. The main risk factor for this name is represented by rates, with credit spreads secondary. Within a normalized market, the ETF is slated for a +7% total return in 2025, with most of the figure coming from dividends.

data.title: Lucid: Improving Growth Outlook
data.description:
Electric vehicle maker Lucid Group (NASDAQ:LCID) reported better-than-expected earnings for Q1’25 and saw a considerable uptick in deliveries in the first-quarter as well. Lucid generated double-digit delivery and revenue growth in Q1’25 and the company narrowed its losses in the last quarter as well. Lucid is embarking on an aggressive expansion strategy and confirmed its delivery target of approximately 20,000 units in FY 2025. Given its relatively low annual production and delivery volume, I am still cautious about Lucid, but could see an upside revaluation scenario in case the EV company ramps up deliveries in the years ahead.

Previous rating

I rated shares of the electric vehicle maker a hold in February -- Likely Fairly Valued -- because the EV company was generating significant losses and didn't make as much progress as I had hoped in terms of improving its profitability profile. Further, Lucid disappointed a number of times in the past in terms of its execution against its growth targets (revised outlooks) which caused me to have a low degree of confidence in the company's delivery projections. However, Lucid has a catalyst in its business, which is the launch of the Gravity SUV, the company’s second EV production vehicle after the Lucid Air, last year.

Lucid beat EPS estimates

Lucid had adjusted earnings of $(0.20) per-share in Q1'25, which beat the average prediction by $0.03 per-share. Revenue, however, missed analysts’ expectations by $11.1M and came in at $235.1M.

In terms of delivery achievements, the first quarter was not a bad one for the electric vehicle company: Lucid delivered 3,109 EVs, showing a 58% year-over-year increase. Total revenue for the first-quarter was $235M, showing an increase of 36% compared to the same period last year.

The most important take-away from the company’s earnings release last week was that Lucid confirmed its manufacturing guidance for the full-year: it continues to expect 20,000 EV deliveries, which implies a ~100% increase year-over-year (Lucid delivered 10,241 vehicles in 2024).

This (confirmed) guidance also contrasted nicely with Rivian Automotive (RIVN) which lowered its estimated delivery volume for FY 2025 at the beginning of the month to a new range of 40,000 to 46,000 EVs (previously: 46,000 to 51,000 vehicles).

To achieve this delivery target, Lucid expects to go on an aggressive expansion campaign in 2025 that includes the expansion of its studio and services network, especially in North America and Europe. Lucid also recently added a number of senior management members to its team -- in Engineering, Autonomous Vehicles and Operations (among others) -- which highlights the company’s focus on growth.

A key catalyst for Lucid is the launch of the Gravity SUV, which rolled out in December 2024 and for which the company already opened orders in Canada and Saudi Arabia. Additionally, Lucid has guided for the beginning of production of its third production car, which is set to kick off in mid-2026.

Lucid's deliveries and revenues were up-trending in the most recent quarter at double-digits, but the EV maker also made progress in lowering its operating expenditures, despite the start of Lucid Gravity SUV production: total operating expenses fell 7% year-over-year to $463.4M. In Q1’25, the EV start-up also reported a net loss of $366.2M (before the accretion of convertible preferred stock), showing a decline of 46% year-over-year.

Cash and liquidity run-way

When it comes to EV companies, especially start-ups, one of the most important metrics to pay attention to is cash. Start-ups that have not yet grown into a significant production and delivery volume are typically burning through cash, which is reflected in a constant need to raise capital. As of the end of the March quarter, Lucid had $4.5B in cash and investments stashed on its balance sheet, which showed a decline rate of 10% compared to the end of FY 2024.

Lucid's total liquidity position was slightly higher, at $5.76B, which includes other sources of credit such as term loan credit facilities. Lucid had about $589.9M in negative free cash flow in the first-quarter which calculates to a negative estimate FCF, on an annualized level, of $2.36B. Therefore, Lucid has enough cash and investments on its balance sheet to finance its EV production well into FY 2026. Based off of run-rate FCF, I calculate that Lucid has a liquidity run-way at least up to the third-quarter of next year. It is, however, likely that the EV maker will raise additional equity capital from its main backer, Saudi Arabia's sovereign wealth fund, in 2025 in order to compensate for its cash burn and maintain a protective cushion of cash on its balance sheet.

Lucid’s valuation

Compared to other large cap U.S.-based electric vehicle companies, Lucid has a relatively high valuation, which I do not believe is justified. Lucid is valued at a forward price-to-revenue ratio of 2.9X, which compares to a 2.3X revenue multiplier for Rivian Automotive.

I calculated a fair value for Lucid's shares of $2.00 in my last work on Lucid in February, based off of a P/S ratio of ~3.0X which, I felt, was appropriate for the EV start-up given its past problems meeting its delivery guidance (shares of Lucid have traded at much higher revenue multipliers in 2024).

While I see some positive trends in Lucid's business -- double-digit growth in deliveries and revenue, lower operating expenses (net losses) and progress in terms of expanding production to other EV models -- I am still cautious about Lucid's ability to execute on its growth plans. I also have a hold rating on Rivian Automotive, despite its improved gross margin profile, mainly because the EV maker also watered down its delivery guidance lately.

The EV start-up disappointed more than once in 2022 and 2023 with its deliveries, and I need to see more productive improvements in the company's manufacturing to change my rating on the company. I recognize, however, that Lucid's delivery growth accelerated lately, which creates upside in case the EV company can sustain this momentum. Right now, I am still sticking with my hold rating on Lucid.

Risks with Lucid

The biggest risk, as I perceive it, is that the EV maker still has a relatively low annual production and delivery volume, and is still very much in the ramp-up phase for its first production car, the Lucid Air. It may take many more years for the EV maker to actually turn a profit in its electric vehicle manufacturing business, which could weigh on investors’ patience. Shares of Lucid are also highly valued, relative to Rivian Automotive. What would change my mind about Lucid is if the company were to lower its losses, ramp deliveries of its EV products, and achieve its delivery guidance for FY 2025.

Final thoughts

Lucid submitted a decent earnings sheet for the first fiscal quarter on May 6, 2025, which highlighted that the company is making progress in terms of ramping up deliveries. The EV start-up is also growing its revenues at double-digits and, most importantly, narrowing its operating (NET) losses. Lucid is currently a hold for me, despite the considerable ramp in deliveries and revenue, mainly because the EV start-up disappointed in the past with its growth and expansion plans. The high pile of cash and investments on Lucid's balance sheet and strong liquidity position, on the other hand, work in the EV company's favor and give Lucid a long liquidity run-way.

data.title: PennantPark Investment: Q2 Earnings Indicate Continued Weakness
data.description:
Overview

Business Development Companies continue to be one of the most efficient vehicles to capitalize on the higher interest rate environment. However, these unusual times have revealed that not all BDCs are constructed with the same quality. PennantPark Investment (NYSE:PNNT) recently reported their Q2 earnings and I wanted to take this as an opportunity to revisit the fund and assess the performance and outlook. I previously covered PNNT and issued a hold rating due to the lack of NAV growth and fact that net investment income levels didn't fully support the distribution.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that PNNT's price has fallen by more than 8.6%, with most of that decline happening over the month because of the market uncertainty related to President Trump's tariff proposals. When including all distributions paid out over the same time frame, the total return is barely positive at 2.4%. The main appeal of maintaining a position in a BDC like PNNT is the generous starting dividend yield of about 14.7%. As an added bonus, PNNT pays its distributions out on a monthly basis.

Despite the recent price decline, I still don't think that PNNT trades at an attractive valuation yet. Following the recent earnings, I believe there could still be additional downside for shareholders, since the NAV has not grown. Sales and repayments continue to outpace capital allocated towards new investment activity as well. There's a lot that can go wrong from this point forward, so it's crucial that management makes the best strategic moves for PNNT going forward. So let's first take a look at the most recent earnings report and the overall risk profile.

Q2 Earnings & Risk Profile

PNNT recently reported their Q2 earnings and the results were a bit disappointing. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.18 per share, which was a decrease from the prior quarter's net investment income of $0.20 per share. Total investment income was $30.6M ,which was also a reduction from the prior year's amount of $35.9M. At least PNNT was able to slightly reduce expenses down to $19.2M to help offset the lower earnings for the quarter. Net investment income for the quarter declined to $11.4M, from a prior amount of $14.2M. The earnings continue to disappoint and lead to a lower NAV.

  • Q1 2025: $7.56 per share
  • Q2 2025: $7.48 per share

A declining NAV is one of the clearest indications that an underlying strategy isn't effective at generating a sufficient amount of income. It can indicate that management isn't actively growing its base assets enough through new investment activity, or it can hint that the distribution may be too generous. Therefore, I personally require the NAV growth to improve over the remainder of 2025 before my stance on PNNT changes.

In more positive news, PNNT was able to allocate $176.8M towards new investment activity. This capital was allocated towards three new portfolio companies and fifty-two existing portfolio companies with a weighted average yield of 10.7%. Unfortunately, this investment capital was outpaced by sales and repayments totaling $263.1M, ultimately leaving PNNT with a loss of net assets over the quarter. In order for PNNT's fundamental strength to improve, it needs to allocate a lot more capital towards new investments.

I am a bit cautious as I don't feel confident in many BDCs' ability to efficiently allocate new investment capital at the moment. The higher interest rate environment has made it difficult to find attractive deals in the market, so slowing asset growth is a trend we are seeing across the BDC sector right now. I am worried that management may start to loosen their investment criteria, effectively sacrificing quality underwriting just to pursue growth.

I believe that one of the most important aspects to measuring the risk profile of a BDC is to monitor the non-accrual rate. Non-accruals represent the rate of borrowers that are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. As a result, these borrowers on non-accrual status aren't actively contributing towards a BDC's earnings and they reduce the overall quality of the portfolio. When I previously covered PNNT, non-accruals amounted to 4.3% of cost and 1.5% of fair value.

The latest reporting indicates that non-accruals have substantially improved. Non-accruals now sit at 1.6% of cost and 0.4% of fair portfolio value. This non-accrual rate is very competitive when putting it against some peer BDCs in the sector. However, non-accruals are only one piece of the pie and a low rate may not necessarily mean that a BDC will outperform. Just as an example, here are the non-accrual rates for peer BDCs and their respective performance on a YTD basis.

  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 0.9% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • Saratoga Investment Corp. (SAR): 0.3% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • SLR Investment (SLRC): 0.6% non-accrual rate at fair value.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent portfolio breakdown, PNNT has assets totaling $1.2B that is spread across a range of different debt investments. We can see that diversity is a major focus of PNNT's strategy. Financials are estimated to account for the largest slice of assets with a weight of 21.5%. This is followed by varying weights to Business Services, Consumer Products, Healthcare & Pharma, and Transportation just to name a few.

However, I don't really prefer the investment structure that PNNT focuses on. For instance, only 41% of its debt investments are structured on a first lien senior secured basis. First lien debt sits at the top of the capital structure and is my preferred form of debt because it has the highest priority for repayment. This structure can act as a defensive layer for BDCs because it reduces the probability that all invested capital is lost in a bad transaction. If a borrower defaults and needs to liquidate assets, PNNT would be entitled for repayment before other forms of debt. The entire portfolio breakdown by type is as follows:

  • 41% First Lien Secured Debt
  • 29% Preferred and Common Equity
  • 18% Subordinated Debt
  • 10% US Government Securities
  • 2% Second Lien Secured Debt

The large focus on equity investments increases the risk profile since equity sits on the bottom of the corporate capital structure and has the lowest priority for repayment. Therefore, the equity layer is where losses are most typically absorbed. Equity investments may not produce consistent levels of cash flow, so they depend on the valuation growth of underlying borrowers. This structure allows PNNT to also experience capital appreciation during periods where performance is strong.

Lastly, 91% of the portfolio is structured on a variable rate basis. In theory, this should allow PNNT to capitalize from the higher interest rate environment. A higher interest rate directly translates to a greater amount of interest income that can be collected from its borrowers. However, a higher interest rate can also put stress on any portfolio companies that are already underperforming.

Updated Valuation

Despite the price decline in recent months, PNNT still trades at a smaller discount than usual. For instance, PNNT currently trades at a discount to NAV of about 13.8%. Over the last three year period, PNNT was traded at an average discount to NAV of about 18.14%. What stands out to me within the BDC sector, is that high quality BDCs tend to consistently trade at a premium valuation while lower performers stay in the discount territory. At the moment, I think the recent quarterly reports justify the discounted valuation. Therefore, I don't believe this to be an opportunity, but instead as a sign of the underlying weakness.

Wall Street analysts have an average price target of $6.75 per share. This indicates a small potential of about 1.6% from the current level but I don't think this is anything to get excited about. While I do certainly think that PNNT's price could rebound back up to this price target, I just don't see any large catalysts that can drive growth.

I do think that PNNT could be more successful in changing its growth trajectory in a lower interest rate environment. A lower interest rate could possibly lead to lower net investment income levels, since borrowers will see a reduction in their interest expense being paid. Additionally, a lower interest rate environment may stimulate an environment of increased borrowing activity since it becomes more affordable for businesses to hold debt on their balance sheet. An influx of new opportunities could be exactly what PNNT needs to fuel a greater amount of new investment activity.

Unfortunately, I don't think we are likely to see any sizeable interest rate cuts happen anytime soon. The Fed decided to leave rates unchanged in their most recent meeting as they observe economic data. Markets are still trying to determine what the ripple effects of President Trump's tariff policies will be, inflationary data remains higher than anticipated, unemployment continues to trend upward, and consumer spending habits are shifting. Even if the Fed slightly reduces rates, I don't think it will be enough to drive the needle higher for PNNT, since rates still remain near decade-highs.

Questionable Dividend

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.08 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 14.7%. If we multiply this monthly payout by three, we get a quarterly payout of $0.24 per share. As mentioned, net investment income for the quarter amounted to $0.18 per share. This means that the distribution is not currently being fully supported by the actual earnings of PNNT. I personally think this will eventually lead to a distribution cut, as it makes the most sense. A cut would allow the NAV to grow and create healthier fundamentals for PNNT.

However, management seems confident that they'll eventually be able to support the distribution with net investment income. PNNT has been leaning heavily on its undistributed spillover income to make the shortcoming in earnings. PNNT still has spillover income to support two years worth of distributions but I question whether it's actually worth it to maintain exposure to a BDC that needs to rely on spillover income. This could possibly be a case of management kicking the can down the road and delaying the inevitable cut, but time will eventually tell.

We are pleased that our secured loan portfolio, with among the lowest portfolio company leverage and most meaningful covenants in the industry, is positioned defensively and continues to perform well. Additionally, our dividend stream is supported by substantial spillover income as we look to rotate equity investments over time. - Art Penn, CEO

Management may be waiting for interest rates to get reduced in the hopes of this presenting additional opportunities for growth. I would like to see improvements here eventually so that net investment income can exceed the distributions but given the current circumstances, I am not initiating a position yet.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on PNNT as the latest Q2 earnings left a lot to be desired. Net investment income levels slightly decreased, as well as the NAV per share. Sales and repayments outpaced new investment activity, also causing a decline in net assets. The earnings still don't fully support the distribution and this is one of my main concerns. Management believes that they'll eventually get earnings back above the distributions but I remain cautious. I don't typically prefer to entrust a BDC that is reliant on spillover income to support consistent dividend income within my portfolio. While the recent price drop could certainly be an opportunity for patient investors to accumulate, I prefer to wait on the sidelines for now.
